tweets.text:
MarketWatch: Stuart Kaiser, head of U.S. equity trading strategy at Citi, says "the oil tail risk outcome has been cut by 3/4 from about the same starting price," since March 27th.

On March 27, when the U.S. Oil Fund was trading above $124, options implied a 6.2% chance it would rally a further 50% in a month. 

Now, with $USO near $128, odds of a 50% gain are just 1.5%.

tweets.text:
Goldman: In Exhibit 1, we plot our preferred guidepost for economic activity for the S&P 500: revenues excluding the volatile and idiosyncratic energy sector, converted to real terms using the GDP price deflator (ex-energy). 

On this basis, real revenues rose 6.3% year-over-year in Q1, the fastest pace since 2021 and near the top-end of the range seen last cycle.

The current pace of S&P 500 revenue growth is elevated relative to GDP growth, even after accounting for the fact that the S&P 500 is more cyclical than the broader economy—the historical relationship between the two suggests that the 2.6% year-over-year increase in real GDP in Q1 is consistent with S&P 500 real revenue growth just below 4%. 

The current outperformance reflects that there has been particularly strong revenue growth among the largest public companies—real revenues have increased by a more moderate 5% over the last year for the median S&P 500 company—and especially the large tech companies—real revenues have increased by 2% for the S&P 500 excluding the tech and energy sectors.
tweets.full_text:
Goldman: In Exhibit 1, we plot our preferred guidepost for economic activity for the S&P 500: revenues excluding the volatile and idiosyncratic energy sector, converted to real terms using the GDP price deflator (ex-energy). 

On this basis, real revenues rose 6.3% year-over-year in Q1, the fastest pace since 2021 and near the top-end of the range seen last cycle.

The current pace of S&P 500 revenue growth is elevated relative to GDP growth, even after accounting for the fact that the S&P 500 is more cyclical than the broader economy—the historical relationship between the two suggests that the 2.6% year-over-year increase in real GDP in Q1 is consistent with S&P 500 real revenue growth just below 4%. 

The current outperformance reflects that there has been particularly strong revenue growth among the largest public companies—real revenues have increased by a more moderate 5% over the last year for the median S&P 500 company—and especially the large tech companies—real revenues have increased by 2% for the S&P 500 excluding the tech and energy sectors.

tweets.text:
Goldman edged their already lower 2Q GDP estimate down a smaller tenth of a percent following Monday's data to +1.9%.

As a reminder, I do a summary each week in the Week Ahead (https://t.co/dK8IKSbarp) of six major GDP trackers which was 2.40% (avg) coming into the week. With the changes it  pulls the average down to +2.25%, the median remains at +2.50%: 

BoA (who has been the most accurate over the past year) +2.5% (from +2.6% the prior week).

Goldman +2.0% (unch) now +1.9%

JPM +2.5% (from +2.0%)

Atlanta Fed +3.82% (+4.29%) now +3.02%

NY Fed +2.50% (+2.61%)

St Louis Fed +1.05% (+1.32%).

Avg = 2.40% (2.44%) now 2.25%

Median = 2.50% (2.20%)
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: The Q2 GDP tracker from the Atlanta Fed was updated Monday incorporating the advanced trade and inventories data from Friday and manufacturing PMIs and construction spending data from yesterday, and the negatives on the weaker than expected inventory build and construction https://t.co/Noz0zBmrTw

tweets.text:
While the result of today's Eurozone CPI reading for May wasn't going to change an ECB rate hike next week, the result will frame the discussion around the path of rates thereafter.

While the headline came in as estimated (+3.2% y/y), core was hot at +2.5% and the closely watched services reading came in at +3.5%.  

"Those moves have been driven by services prices, which have probably been pushed up by pass-through from oil prices. That may be used by the hawks on the Governing Council to argue broad-based inflation requires a follow-up move in September.” —By David Powell, senior euro-area economist.

Data last week showed inflation remaining well above the ECB’s 2% target in all three of the bloc’s biggest member states.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
🇪🇺 #Eurozone Inflation Surpasses 3% for First Time Since 2023 - Bloomberg
https://t.co/U5cIUbmMwg https://t.co/H2GEbTxbuN

tweets.text:
MarketWatch: Based on historical data going back to 1950, the S&P 500 has tended to lose some momentum immediately after nine-week winning streaks (there have been 18, although just two since 1989), but the pattern has looked more favorable over longer stretches, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Following past nine-week streaks, the index was down an average -0.2% one week later, but up 1.1% one month later, 2.4% three months later and 2.9% six months later. The index was higher six months later 61.1% of the time.
tweets.full_text:

tweets.text:
BoA: The Sell Side Indicator (SSI) is a contrarian sentiment signal that tracks sell side strategists’ average recommended allocation to equities in a balanced fund. 

The indicator increased 60bp to 56.2% in May, its highest level since February 2025...  “Neutral”, but tilting toward “Sell"... almost 4x closer to a "Sell" signal than a "Buy" (1.3ppt vs. 4.9ppt). 

Historically, when the indicator has been here or higher, next 12-month S&P 500 returns were negative 32% of the time vs 18% overall since 1985. That said, the current reading of 56.2% is still below levels reached in prior market peaks (typically over 59%) and implies a healthy S&P 500 price return of 12% over the next 12 months.
tweets.full_text:

Historically, when the indicator has been here or higher, next 12-month S&P 500 returns were negative 32% of the time vs 18% overall since 1985. That said, the current reading of 56.2% is still below levels reached in prior market peaks (typically over 59%) and implies a healthy S&P 500 price return of 12% over the next 12 months.
